turns.content: I've been using macros in Java for years, but I'm confused about how Rust's macro_rules! actually works compared to the way I used to write test helpers. Can you explain what happens when I call a macro like this? I want to understand the actual compilation process.

When you invoke a macro like `test_all` in your code, the compiler replaces that entire call site with the expanded body of the macro_rules! definition before any Rust type or trait can be evaluated. So instead of calling a function at runtime, the macro gets inlined directly into your source file as if you had typed out every test case manually. For example, if your macro expands to `assert_eq!(a, b)`, that line disappears from your code and becomes `assert_eq!(1, 2)` right where the call was.

Okay, so it's just text substitution at compile time. But I still have hundreds of tests in my crate, like those generated by rstest, and I'm wondering if macro_rules! is actually capable of handling that volume without exploding my codebase or causing performance issues with the rustc compiler?

You are correct to worry about verbosity. While a single `macro_rules!` definition can be huge, modern compilers handle it well because they only parse definitions once and expand them at call sites. If you have many tests generated by rstest using macros like `test_case`, the expansion is still just normal Rust code that the optimizer sees. The key is avoiding nested macro invocations which can cause exponential growth in file size.

That makes sense, but I'm getting frustrated because my IDE seems to choke when I try to refactor a large macro block and I can't see what's actually being generated inside it like with Java's preprocessor. Is there any way to get better visibility into the expansion without breaking the build?

The frustration is common since Rust doesn't have a built-in expand command in your editor by default, but you can use tools like `cargo-expand` or `rustc -Zunpretty=expanded` which will dump the expanded macro code to stdout as a string. This lets you inspect exactly what types and traits are being instantiated without actually running the tests. It's not as seamless as Java's preprocessor output, but it gives you full control over the generated text.

Alright, I'll try that command next time. One last question: if I'm building a crate with many test functions created by macros, how do I ensure they all get picked up by cargo test and don't end up as orphan items or unused code warnings?

You need to make sure every macro-generated function is declared in your `lib.rs` or main module so the compiler knows about them. If you define a macro that creates functions but doesn't re-export them, cargo will warn about unused definitions. Just ensure the macro expands into regular functions and they are visible in the crate root before running tests.
Thanks for clarifying the visibility rules and the expand command trick. I think I have enough to start experimenting with rstest macros now.
You're welcome! Start small with a few test cases using `macro_rules!` combined with `rstest`, then gradually increase complexity as you get comfortable with the expansion behavior.

I'm trying to generate a bunch of tests for my data structures using the macro system, but I'm stuck on how to handle parameters. Here is what I have so far: macro_rules! test_all { ($val:expr) => { #[test] fn test_val() {{ assert_eq!(ok($val), $val); }} } }; I thought this would work like a standard function call where the macro takes an argument and produces one test case per value, but it feels wrong to me.

You are correct that `macro_rules!` creates zero-cost code expansion rather than runtime execution. The issue is that your current definition generates a single test function named `test_val` that references `$val`, which won't actually run different values unless you expand the macro multiple times manually or use an iterator. To get parametrized tests, you need to iterate over a collection of values inside the macro expansion itself.

Ah okay so I should just loop in my test function? But wait, if I do that, isn't this defeating the purpose of using macros for code generation? My goal is to avoid writing `test_one`, `test_two`, etc. manually.

You hit on a key distinction: macros are meant to generate many test functions automatically, not just wrap existing logic in a loop. You can combine this with `rstest` crate which provides the `rstest::fixture!` macro to manage test fixtures and parameterized values cleanly without writing repetitive boilerplate.
That sounds promising, but I don't understand why `macro_rules!` alone isn't enough here. Can't I just write a regular function that takes an iterator and generates the tests?

A normal function runs at runtime and cannot generate new functions at compile time, which is what you need for test generation. `macro_rules!` expands to actual code before compilation so each iteration creates a distinct test function with its own name and logic. You can combine this with `proc_macro::TokenStream` if you needed more complex behavior like recursive expansion or custom formatting.

Got it, so the pattern is to use a macro that iterates over values in its body and then call that macro multiple times? That seems right. I think I understand how `macro_rules!` interacts with `impl Test` traits now.

Exactly, you define a macro that expands to repeated test function definitions, and then call it with different values. This avoids the verbosity of manual test functions while keeping tests organized and maintainable under the `Test` trait requirements.

I'm struggling to generate hundreds of test cases for my serialization logic without writing repetitive code. I tried using a loop in main.rs but it feels wrong compared to the idiomatic approach, and I can't find where to put the macro definition.

You are likely looking at how to use the macro_rules! syntax inside your lib.rs or main.rs file. For generating tests specifically, you should combine this with the rstest crate since it integrates well with Rust's testing framework and provides traits like TestArguments that make macro expansion easier.

I see how I can use the derive attribute on the test function itself, but my custom macro is still expanding into too many lines. Can you show me a concrete example of using the macro_rules! syntax to generate cases for different integer values?
I need to clarify if your input data comes from an external configuration file or is hardcoded in the test module, because that changes whether you should use a loop or a macro.
